text:
he increasing global emphasis on sustainable develop-
ent has brought quality education to the forefront,
s highlighted by the United Nations Educational, Sci-
ntific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO) and its
7 Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), which in-
lude goals,  such as equitable education,  sustainable
ommunities, and reduced inequalities. Among the key

.1 Causal Graph
hen recognizing learning paths, it is essential to deter-
ine the relationships among KCs within a KC graph
20]. Most KCs exhibit causal dependencies, which can
e effectively represented using a causal graph—a Di-
ected Acyclic Graph (DAG) that models the connec-
ions between phenomena and their underlying causes.
method for constructing causal knowledge networks
ased on Bayesian Networks (BNs) has been proposed,
everaging causal relationships to build these structures
38].
The application of causal graphs in educational re-
earch has been widely acknowledged, offering key ad-

metadata.section_title: MAS-KCL for KC Graph Structure Learning

text:
.1 LLM-based Multi-Agent System
his section introduces the multi-agent system com-
osed of AI agents, which dynamically control param-
ters to deliver decision outputs during the algorithm’s
terative process. These agents  interact with MAS-
CL by influencing the optimization process through
ecisions continuously updated via LLM calls. The
verall interaction between agents and the MAS-KCL-
ontrolled population is illustrated in Figure 2. In ad-
ition, the pseudocode of the proposed method is pro-
ided in Algorithm 1.
Specifically, after each iteration of the algorithm,
he Game Agent is invoked using prompt engineering.
t is asked to determine whether to adjust the Am-
ient Pressure (AP) parameter in MAS-KCL—based
n the magnitude of loss change observed in the cur-
ent iteration—and to what extent. The decision is re-
urned in a structured JSON format, which includes

Fig. 2 Game agent and feedback agents. The diagram displays the i
as the probability of adding new edges in the bidirectional feedback m
both the decision outcome and its reasoning. Before  s
the next iteration begins, the value of AP is updated  T
accordingly, allowing the algorithm to remain dynami-  k
cally aligned with the intermediate decisions made by  e
the Game Agent, as described in Algorithm 1. The value  m
of the AP parameter determines the proportion of elite  d
individuals retained for the next iteration. It reflects the  a
dynamic allocation of computational effort between ex-  a
ploration and exploitation.  m
Similarly, the Positive Feedback Agent and Nega-
tive Feedback Agent are invoked at the end of each it-  g
eration. Based on the current loss change and parame-  o
ter status, they make decisions regarding the activation  E
and adjustment of the positive and negative feedback  o
mechanisms,  respectively. These decisions take effect  g
in the subsequent iteration and are passed to MAS-  a
KCL through dynamic parameter control, as described  s
in Lines 20–21 of Algorithm 1. The Positive Feedback  i
Agent focuses on identifying edges that contribute to  a
increased loss and aims to eliminate these low-quality  s
edges broadly across the population. In contrast, the  p
Negative Feedback Agent is responsible for identifying  i
edges associated with decreased loss and promotes the  T
widespread addition of these valuable edges through-  f
out the population. Together, these agents collaborate  a
to enable efficient learning of the KC graph structure,
thereby supporting teachers  in making informed in-  u
structional interventions, as illustrated in Figure 1. Fur-
P
ther details on the interaction between the multi-agent  (
system and the MAS-KCL framework are provided in
t
the following Section 3.2.  (
i
s
3.2 The Structure of the Proposed MAS-KCL  p
i
To improve the balance between exploration and ex-  a
ploitation, the proposed MAS-KCL adopts a multi-sub-  fi
population strategy that partitions the population into  s

nteraction between the Game Agent and the population, as well

uperior, exploratory, and elimination sub-populations.
his  structure allows certain individuals  to exploit
nown promising regions while others explore new ar-
as, enhancing global search capacity and avoiding pre-
ature convergence. The algorithm evaluates each in-
ividual using a fitness score F , with the loss defined
s 1 − F [20]. A higher F indicates better structural
lignment and, consequently, a lower loss, reflecting a
ore effective solution.
The main process of the algorithm is detailed in Al-
orithm 1. The procedure begins with the initialization
f a random population and the setting of the Function
valuation (FE) counter to zero, as shown in Lines 1-2
f Algorithm 1. In each iteration, DE is employed to
enerate an offspring population, which is then sorted
ccording to a predefined optimization direction, as de-
cribed in Lines 4-5. The offspring are ranked in ascend-
ng order of loss and divided into three sub-populations,
s outlined in Lines 6-7.

metadata.section_title: MAS-KCL for KC Graph Structure Learning

text:
The proportion of the superior
ub-population is controlled by the ambient pressure
arameter AP , where a higher AP preserves more elite
ndividuals, and a lower value encourages exploration.
he exploratory sub-population, sized as (1−AP)·N, is
urther refined through a bidirectional feedback mech-
nism, as implemented in Lines 7-15 of Algorithm 1.
Within this  feedback mechanism,  individuals are
pdated based on their sub-population assignments:
PFO (high-fitness exploratory individuals) and PNFO
remaining individuals) influence the addition or dele-
ion of  edges through positive and negative  factors
PF , NF). These structural changes are quantified us-
ng the metric count ones. The modified exploratory
ub-population is then merged with the superior sub-
opulation and passed to the next generation, as shown
n Line 17 of Algorithm 1. Following this, both parent
nd offspring populations are re-ranked, and the least
t individuals are eliminated to maintain selection pres-
ure and solution quality, as described in Lines 16-17..

.1 Experimental Setup
.1.1 Educational Datasets
he experiments in this study are mainly conducted
sing datasets from the NeurIPS CausalML Challenge
nd the XuetangX platform, both of which are suitable
or the task of knowledge component graph structure
earning. A total of 9 datasets are used in this research,
ncluding 5 synthetic datasets and 4 real-world datasets.
etailed descriptions of the datasets are provided below
ccording to their respective sources.
• Learning  Path  Recognition-Generate
Datasets  (LPR-GDs)  [9]:  The  LPR-GDs
series  datasets  are  provided  by  the NeurIPS
CausalML Challenge: Causal Insights for Learning
Paths in Education. This series includes five gen-
erated datasets—LPR-GD1 to LPR-GD5—which
contain data on the causal  relationships among
KCs  in  the domain of mathematics. Although

6
there exists a gap between synthetic and real-world
data,  the LPR-GDs datasets are well-suited for  a
the preliminary validation and rapid testing of KC  t
graph structure learning methods.  t
• Learning  Path  Recognition-Real  World  o
Datasets (LPR-RWDs)  [8]:  The LPR-RWDs  r
series  datasets  provided by Microsoft Research  b
Cambridge  and Eedi.  The LPR-RWDs  include  n
three real-world datasets of varying scales: LPR-  j
RWD,  LPR-RWD1,  and  LPR-RWD2.  These
datasets are constructed from real-world educa-  4
tional data provided by Eedi, an online education
company registered in England and Wales. The  T
LPR-GDs series datasets represent  learning data  c
collected  from real  learners  engaged  in mathe-  d
matics  education, making  them well-suited  for  R
thoroughly evaluating the generalizability of KC  M
graph structure learning methods.  d
• MOOCCubeX-Math  [43]:  XuetangX is one  p
of  the  largest MOOC platforms  in China. The  s
MOOCCubeX dataset  is  collected on the Xue-  m
tangX platform, which provides partially anno-
tated and algorithmically predicted prerequisite re-
lationships between concepts [44]. By treating the  4
concepts  defined  in MOOCCubeX as KCs, we
T
can obtain real-world data suitable  for the task
g
of KC graph structure  learning. Specifically, we
o
extracted MOOCCubeX-Math  from the 331,202
t
mathematics-related JSON records  provided by
2
MOOCCubeX and performed data cleaning based
on the methodology in [30], in order to further eval-
w
uate the generalizability of the proposed approach.

4.1.2 Baseline Algorithms
u
s
In this study, we compare our proposed algorithm,
a
MAS-KCL, against four representative baseline algo-
l
rithms: Multi-Stage multi-objective Evolutionary Al-
l
gorithm (MSEA) [34], Golden Eagle Optimizer (GEO)
i
[24], Elite Evolution Strategy-based Harris Hawk Opti-
a
mization algorithm (EESHHO) [11], and Adaptive Ge-
O
ometry Estimation-based Many-Objective Evolution-
s
ary Algorithm II (AGE-MOEA-II) [25]. The selected
p
methods exhibit diverse characteristics, making them
c
suitable for a comprehensive comparison and evalua-
g
tion of the proposed MAS-KCL from multiple perspec-
tives. Our selection was guided by the recommenda-
tions in [20], from which MSEA, GEO, and EESHHO  4
were adopted. Additionally, we included AGE-MOEA-
II, a recently proposed algorithm with strong theoreti-  T
cal foundations, to further validate the effectiveness of  p
the proposed LLM-driven approach.  s

†
Yuan-Hao Jiang  et al.
It is worth noting that the parameter settings for
ll  comparison algorithms were either derived from
heir original papers or based on the default configura-
ions provided by the Platform for Evolutionary Multi-
bjective Optimization (PlatEMO) [33]. The hyperpa-
ameters of the comparison algorithms are listed in Ta-
le 1. It should be noted that AGE-MOEA-II [25]  is
ot included in Table 1 because it does not contain ad-
ustable parameters.
.1.3 Implementation Details
he experiments were primarily conducted on a ma-
hine equipped with an Intel  i5-13600KF @ 3.50GHz
ual-core processor, 32GB of RAM, and an NVIDIA
TX 4070 GPU. The software environment included
ATLAB 2022b and the PlatEMO platform [33]. Ad-
itionally, the online tool Chiplot.online was used for
art of the data processing and visualization in this
tudy. Following previous work, we employed the loss
etric to evaluate the performance [20].
.2 Comparison Experiments on Real-World Datasets
his subsection compares the MAS-KCL with four al-
orithms on real-world datasets. To reduce the impact
f random factors, each algorithm was run at least three
imes for each dataset. The results are shown in Table
, where lower loss indicates better performance.
Table  2  presents  the  results  on  four  real-
orld datasets: MOOCCubeX-Math, LPR-RWD, LPR-
WD1, and LPR-RWD2, with problem sizes D of 210,
670,  1225,  and 1225,  respectively. The results  for
ach algorithm are reported as the average loss val-
es obtained through multiple independent runs.

As
hown in this table, LPR-RWD exhibits the highest loss
mong the three LPR-RWDs series datasets due to its
argest problem size, making optimization more chal-
enging. Meanwhile,  the MOOCCubeX-Math dataset
s relatively sparse, which leads to poorer performance
cross most algorithms compared to the other datasets.
verall, the EESHHO algorithm consistently delivers
econd-best performance across all datasets, while the
roposed MAS-KCL achieves the best results in every
ase, demonstrating its effectiveness in the task of KC
raph structure learning.
.3 Generalization Comparison on Generated Datasets
o further evaluate the generalization capability of the
roposed method, we conducted additional compari-
on experiments on the generated datasets. Specifically,

MAS-KCL was compared against the best-performing
baseline algorithm identified in Section 4.2. The re-  l
sults on five generated datasets—LPR-GD1 to LPR-  d
GD5—are presented in Table 3. Consistent with the  n
results observed on real-world datasets, MAS-KCL  a
achieves  the best performance across all  generated  t
datasets. On average, MAS-KCL reduces the loss by  m
5.51%, with the greatest  improvement observed on  c
LPR-GD3, where the reduction reaches 7.71%. These  i
results confirm the strong generalization ability of the  K
MAS-KCL on both real-world and generated datasets.
4
4.4 Generalization Sensitivity of MAS-KCL to LLMs  T
a
Since MAS-KCL  integrates  LLMs  to  enhance  its  a
decision-making process, we conducted a comparative  a
study to analyze its generalization performance under  s
different LLM versions. As shown in Table 4, we com-  t
pared two GPT models developed by OpenAI (GPT-  a
4.0 and GPT-3.5) with two alternative LLMs (LLaMA-  p
70B and Claude-3-7-Sonnet-20250219) on four  real-  a
world datasets. The results show that the GPT mod-  t
els consistently outperform the other LLMs across all  p
datasets. Notably, GPT-3.5 performs better on smaller-
scale datasets such as MOOCCubeX-Math, while GPT-  c
4.0 exhibits superior performance as the problem size  r
increases, as seen in the LPR-RWD dataset. On aver-  r
age, the use of GPT models reduces the loss by 5.79%  i
compared to other LLMs, demonstrating their strong  K
compatibility with the MAS-KCL framework.  i

four real-world datasets. Symbols ”+”, ”=”, and ”–” indicate
st results are shown in bold and underlined, respectively.
Based on these observations, we suggest the fol-
owing: GPT-3.5 should be prioritized for small-scale
atasets; GPT-4.0 is more suitable for large-scale sce-
arios to achieve optimal results. When GPT models
re not available, LLaMA serves as a viable alterna-
ive, offering the lowest standard deviation among all
odels, which indicates better stability—albeit at the
ost of some performance. These findings offer valuable
nsights into selecting LLMs for integration with MAS-
CL in different application contexts.
.5 Ablation Experiments
o assess the contribution of  the LLM-based multi-
gent system to algorithm performance, we conducted
n ablation study by removing the MAS component
nd evaluating the results on the real-world LPR-RWDs
eries datasets, as shown in Figure 4. Through itera-
ive optimization, the standard MAS-KCL consistently
chieved significantly lower loss across all datasets com-
ared to MAS-KCL(-MAS), which excludes the multi-
gent system component. These findings demonstrate
hat integrating the multi-agent system component im-
roves the efficiency of KC graph structure learning.
To further investigate the impact of each agent, we
onducted a more fine-grained ablation study, and the
esults are presented in Table 5. We observed that the
emoval of any single agent led to an increase in loss,
ndicating that each agent contributes positively to the
C graph structure learning task.

Specifically, remov-
ng the entire multi-agent system resulted in a 10.63%.
